Environmental Impact Statement, Central Arizona Project

The Central Arizona Project (CAP) aims to transport water from Lake Havasu to central Arizona, including Phoenix and Tucson, through a system of aqueducts, dams, and pumping stations. The project is designed to provide water for municipal, industrial, and agricultural uses in Arizona and parts of New Mexico. While it promises to address water shortages, it raises concerns about environmental impacts, including the alteration of natural landscapes, effects on wildlife, and potential harm to Native American lands and water rights.
Central Arizona, including Phoenix and Tucson, and parts of New Mexico
League of Women Voters of Arizona

The Central Arizona Project was proposed to address the growing water demands in central Arizona, particularly in urban areas like Phoenix and Tucson, as well as in parts of New Mexico. There was significant public concern regarding the environmental impacts, including effects on wildlife and Native American lands. Despite the pushback, the project was ultimately completed and continues to play a crucial role in water supply for the region.
"The destruction of intensely used recreation areas on the Salt and Verde Rivers; the adverse effect on the Yavapai-Apache people of the Fort McDowell Reservation." — Public Comments – Sierra Club
"The CAP is a dubious project, being questioned by reputable economists who have studied it thoroughly." — Public Comments – Frank Welsh, P.E.
